title: Washington Vets AI Models at the Gate, Beijing Squeezes the Minerals Pipeline
subhead: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 to government-approved partners, Anthropic gets a partial reprieve for Mythos 5, China's Zhipu closes the frontier gap with open-source GLM 5.2 — while the Entity List sits empty for eight months as rare earth leverage holds.

sections.heading: OpenAI Staggers GPT-5.6 Release After White House Request
sections.body:
OpenAI launched three new models — GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna — on Friday but said it is complying with a Trump administration request to initially restrict access to a small group of trusted partners preapproved by the US government. The company said it previewed the models' capabilities and shared release plans with officials ahead of launch. OpenAI stated it 'doesn't believe this kind of government access process should become the long-term default' but called the phased release the 'strongest path to broader availability in the coming weeks.'

GPT-5.6 Sol is OpenAI's strongest model yet, with improvements in coding, biology, and cybersecurity. OpenAI said Sol is better at helping users fix vulnerabilities than at carrying out end-to-end attacks, and does not cross its 'critical' cybersecurity risk threshold. The company has not disclosed which roughly 20 customers have been approved so far. According to WIRED, OpenAI sends the government a list and receives feedback on who can access the model — a process with no established framework yet.

The move comes two weeks after Anthropic was forced to take its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models offline under a Commerce Department export control directive. President Trump signed an AI executive order earlier in June creating a 'voluntary process' for labs to share models with the government 30 days before release — but OpenAI executives told reporters that no such voluntary framework actually exists yet, leaving frontier labs in what WIRED described as 'a very weird interim period.'
sections.whyItMatters: The US government is now effectively gating frontier AI model releases in real time, without established rules or legislation — creating a de facto licensing regime from an executive order that explicitly promised not to become one.

sections.heading: Anthropic Gets Partial Reprieve as Mythos 5 Returns to Select Partners
sections.body:
The Trump administration partially reversed its Anthropic export control directive on Friday, allowing Mythos 5 to be redeployed to more than 100 US government agencies and companies that operate and defend critical infrastructure. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick wrote to Anthropic's chief compute officer Tom Brown: 'I have determined that appropriate safeguards are in place to permit certain trusted partners to access the Claude Mythos 5 Model,' according to Semafor. Non-American employees at those organizations are now also permitted to access the model.

However, Fable 5 remains offline. Fable 5 was Anthropic's publicly available version of Mythos with additional safety guardrails, but both models were pulled after security researchers easily bypassed those protections. Anthropic said it will 'continue to work with the government to expand access to Mythos 5 and make Fable 5 available for general use again.'

Stanford cybersecurity expert Alex Stamos pushed back hard on the government's actions, telling reporters: 'Pretty much nobody in the cybersecurity industry believes that there's any factual basis for this action.' Stamos said he reviewed Amazon's analysis of Fable and found no risks that aren't present in other publicly available models, including Chinese ones. US Representative Lori Trahan criticized the process: 'No law. No process. No oversight. Just appointees in Washington deciding who's in and who's out.'
sections.whyItMatters: Mythos 5 returns only to government-vetted defenders, not the broader market — Fable 5 stays banned. The asymmetric treatment of two versions of the same model raises questions about whether the review process is about risk or about control.

sections.heading: Zhipu's Open-Source GLM 5.2 Narrows the Frontier Gap
sections.body:
Chinese AI startup Zhipu released GLM 5.2 last week, and the model has quickly become the strongest open-source offering — within a percentage point of Anthropic's Opus 4.8 on agentic benchmarks, at roughly one-fifth the cost, according to CNBC. OpenRouter token traffic for GLM 5.2 climbed faster than it did after DeepSeek's V4 launch in April. Unlike DeepSeek, which the market eventually dismissed as a one-off, GLM 5.2 is strong at agentic work like planning, coding, testing, and looping — the kind of tasks enterprises are racing to automate.

Gabe Pereyra, co-founder of Harvey, told CNBC: 'I've been consistently surprised by how quickly the open source has caught up. GLM 5.2, you're seeing the first model where it's really competitive with some of these closed-source frontier models.' The model is free to download, fine-tune, and run on enterprise servers — a sharp contrast to the same week's events at OpenAI and Anthropic, where government intervention is constraining access.

CNBC frames the convergence: 'The most important metric is becoming intelligence per dollar, making Zhipu's cheap-but-good-enough model an attractive answer.' The timing is not incidental — with US frontier models locked behind government approvals, a model that no government can revoke increasingly looks like the safer bet.
sections.whyItMatters: GLM 5.2 is not just cheap — it's genuinely capable on enterprise-relevant tasks, and it can't be restricted by any government export order. That combination makes open-source models a structural competitor to controlled frontier releases in a way DeepSeek V4 didn't.

sections.heading: Entity List Runs Dry as Blacklisted Names Sit in a Drawer
sections.body:
An interagency committee approved DeepSeek, CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese firms for the US Entity List last year — but the Commerce Department has never published the names. Reuters first reported the backlog this week, and the gap is now the longest since the Entity List became a serious enforcement tool. The US has added nobody to the list in over eight months, since October 2025.

Commerce Under Secretary Jeffrey Kessler has avoided new listings for fear of escalation, according to Yahoo News. The reason: China controls rare earth supplies that US defense, auto, and chip firms cannot replace quickly. The blacklist has effectively become a bargaining chip the Trump administration is holding in reserve for trade negotiations.

The cost is tangible. Some unlisted firms allegedly supplied components for Russian drones recovered in Poland last September. Others sold restricted Nvidia chips into Chinese universities. Anthropic and OpenAI both told US officials that DeepSeek tried to extract capabilities from their models. Meanwhile, Commerce promised to replace a Biden-era rule governing global access to US AI chips but never did — and is not enforcing the old one. The loophole may already be moving chips to Chinese firms operating outside China.
sections.whyItMatters: The Entity List is now a trade-negotiation lever, not a security instrument. Eight months of silence means approved threats are still receiving US technology, and the signal to Beijing is that rare earth leverage buys time on chip restrictions.

sections.heading: SpaceX Signs $6.3B Compute Deal With Reflection AI
sections.body:
SpaceX signed a compute agreement with open-source AI startup Reflection AI worth up to $6.3 billion, according to Reuters. Reflection will pay approximately $150 million per month starting July 1, 2026, with the contract running through 2029. Either side can exit after an early period with notice.

Reflection AI, founded in 2024 by former DeepMind researchers Misha Laskin and Ioannis Antonoglou, raised $2 billion in 2025 at an $8 billion valuation led by Nvidia. The company describes itself as building 'frontier open intelligence' — powerful AI models intended to be more openly accessible than closed systems.

The deal underscores that SpaceX is now functioning as an AI infrastructure provider, not just a space company. Memeburn notes that Google separately agreed to pay SpaceX $920 million per month for AI compute in a larger deal. With Starlink, rocket launches, and now GPU rental, SpaceX's revenue base spans connectivity, transport, and compute — the three pillars of the AI buildout.
sections.whyItMatters: Compute is becoming the bottleneck that determines who can compete in frontier AI. SpaceX turning GPU clusters into a $6.3B revenue stream signals that infrastructure ownership is the new power axis — and open-source AI ambitions still require multibillion-dollar proprietary infrastructure.

sections.heading: SandboxAQ Wins $500M CHIPS Act Award for Domestic Materials
sections.body:
SandboxAQ announced a $500 million award from the US Department of Commerce's CHIPS R&D Office on June 17 to develop domestic alternatives for foreign-controlled materials critical to semiconductor manufacturing. The award covers four programmatic areas: PFAS-free process chemicals, catalysts, rare earth-free magnets, and battery systems. The Department of Commerce will receive a minority, non-voting equity stake in SandboxAQ as part of the deal.

The company's Large Quantitative Models (LQMs) — AI systems trained on physics, chemistry, and biology rather than human language — will virtually screen millions of candidate materials before the most promising are validated in labs. SandboxAQ says its AQCat workflows, built on 13.5 million quantum chemistry calculations developed with NVIDIA, can screen catalyst candidates 20,000 times faster than traditional methods.

The award targets a real vulnerability: China controls more than 90% of global neodymium-based permanent magnet production, and those magnets sit inside every advanced chip printing machine, vacuum pump, and precision actuator. Terbium — one of two heavy rare earths critical for military-grade magnets — is up 103% this year, with non-Chinese sources now trading at three to four times Chinese domestic prices. China's rare earth magnet exports to the US fell 22.5% year-over-year in the first two months of 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: The CHIPS Act is shifting from fab subsidies to materials sovereignty. SandboxAQ's $500M is not about building chips — it's about removing the chemical and mineral dependencies that make chip production vulnerable to Chinese export controls. The question is whether AI-accelerated materials discovery can move fast enough to matter.

sections.heading: Turla's STOCKSTAY Backdoor Targets Ukrainian Government
sections.body:
Google's Threat Intelligence Group detailed a new .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY deployed by Russian state-sponsored actor Turla against Ukrainian government and military organizations, as well as entities with interest in Italian foreign policy. STOCKSTAY uses secure WebSocket C2 communication via the open-source websocket-sharp library, with inter-process communication based on Windows WM_COPYDATA messages.

The malware has four components: MARKETMAKER (downloader), STOCKBROKER (proxy-aware tunneler), STOCKTRADER (main backdoor for info gathering), and STOCKMARKET (orchestrator). STOCKTRADER supports file exfiltration, screen capture, registry manipulation, and remote command execution. Google identified a public GitHub repository containing a Python implementation of the C2 server controller.

STOCKSTAY shares significant code overlap with Kazuar, Turla's long-standing implant since 2017, with suspected development dating back to December 2022. Recent distribution campaigns used phishing emails with malicious RDP files and RAR archives exploiting CVE-2025-8088, a WinRAR vulnerability also exploited by Sandworm, Gamaredon, and RomCom. Turla deploys STOCKSTAY both for initial access into unprofiled environments and for post-exploitation after reconnaissance — a dual-use pattern indicating mature operational tradecraft.
sections.whyItMatters: STOCKSTAY is not a new spy tool — it's a mature, multi-component platform with years of development, showing Russia's sustained investment in Ukrainian targeting even as the war grinds on. The WinRAR CVE-2025-8088 exploitation across multiple Russian groups is a shared tradecraft signal worth monitoring.

bottomLine: Friday June 27 will be remembered as the day the US government moved from threatening AI export controls to actively implementing them — OpenAI released GPT-5.6 only to White House-approved partners, Anthropic's Mythos 5 returned on a leash while Fable 5 stayed banned, and the Entity List sat empty for an eighth straight month as rare earth leverage held. Meanwhile Zhipu's open-source GLM 5.2 proved that the models Washington can gatekeep are not the only frontier in town — and that a model no government can revoke may be the more attractive bet. The compute layer kept consolidating: SpaceX's $6.3B deal with Reflection AI and SandboxAQ's $500M CHIPS Act award both underscore that infrastructure and materials, not just algorithms, are where power is being accumulated.
sources: CNBC, WIRED, TechCrunch, AP News, Semafor, Yahoo News/Reuters, Memeburn, The Hacker News/Google GTIG, SandboxAQ, CNBC (Zhipu/GLM 5.2), PR Connect/The Street (rare earths)
